walkthrough.title: Meet the Two Decks
walkthrough.text: Alright, let's get House Hounds set up. Open the box and you'll find two separate decks. The Worker Deck is the big one — 108 Worker cards plus 8 Boss cards. The Worker cards come in three types: Carpenters, Plumbers, and Electricians. Each Worker has a number on it — 1, 2, or 3 — which represents how skilled they are. The Boss cards have a special orange border. The House Deck is smaller — it has 24 House cards and 6 Inspection cards shuffled together.
walkthrough.image_caption: A 3-value Electricians worker card — the number shows the skill level of the crew

walkthrough.title: Shuffle and Place the Decks
walkthrough.text: Shuffle the Worker Deck — that's all 108 Worker cards and 8 Boss cards together — and place it face-down in the middle of the table. Leave a spot next to it for a discard pile. Now shuffle the House Deck — the 24 House cards and 6 Inspection cards — and place it face-down near the Worker Deck.

walkthrough.title: Set Up the Market
walkthrough.text: Flip House cards one at a time from the House Deck and lay them face-up in a row. You need exactly three House cards showing — this row is called the Market. Here's the thing — if you flip an Inspection card instead of a House, don't put it in the Market. Place it face-up in a separate pile next to the Market called the Inspection Pile, then keep flipping until you've got three actual Houses showing.
walkthrough.image_caption: Shingle Falls — a 10-point House card requiring 2 Carpenters, 1 Plumber, and 1 Electrician

walkthrough.title: Deal Your Starting Hand
walkthrough.text: Deal 9 Worker cards face-down to each player. Pick up your hand and look through it. You've got a mix of Carpenters, Plumbers, Electricians, and maybe a Boss card or two.

walkthrough.title: Build Your Starting Crews
walkthrough.text: Now here's the important part. Choose 4 non-Boss Worker cards from your hand and place them face-up in four separate piles in front of you. Each pile gets exactly one card for now. These four piles are your Crew piles, and they represent the teams of workers you'll send out to fix up houses. Try to get a mix of Carpenters, Plumbers, and Electricians if you can, since different houses need different types of workers. The remaining 5 cards stay in your hand.
walkthrough.image_caption: A 2-value Carpenters card — place workers like this face-up in your Crew piles

walkthrough.title: Check the Table and Start
walkthrough.text: Take a quick look around the table. You should see the Worker Deck and discard pile in the center, the House Deck nearby, three House cards face-up in the Market, and possibly an Inspection Pile. Each player has 4 Crew piles in front of them and 5 cards in hand. The player who most recently built or fixed something in real life goes first. Play moves clockwise from there. Time to start flipping houses!

summary.title: Meet the Two Decks
bullets: Worker Deck: 108 Workers (Carpenters, Plumbers, Electricians, each valued 1-3) + 8 Boss cards
House Deck: 24 House cards + 6 Inspection cards

summary.title: Shuffle and Place the Decks
bullets: Shuffle Worker Deck (Workers + Bosses together), place face-down centrally
Shuffle House Deck (Houses + Inspections together), place face-down nearby
Leave space for a Worker discard pile

summary.title: Set Up the Market
bullets: Flip House cards face-up until exactly 3 Houses are showing = the Market
Inspection cards go to a separate Inspection Pile, not the Market
Keep flipping until 3 actual Houses are revealed

summary.title: Deal Your Starting Hand
bullets: Deal 9 Worker cards to each player
Look at your hand — mix of worker types and possibly Boss cards

summary.title: Build Your Starting Crews
bullets: Choose 4 non-Boss Worker cards from hand
Place each face-up as a separate Crew pile (4 piles, 1 card each)
Try to diversify worker types across piles
Keep remaining 5 cards in hand

summary.title: Check the Table and Start
bullets: Center: Worker Deck + discard, House Deck, 3-House Market, Inspection Pile
Each player: 4 Crew piles face-up, 5 cards in hand
First player: whoever most recently built or fixed something
Play clockwise

walkthrough.title: Your Turn — Three Steps
walkthrough.text: Every turn follows the same three steps, always in this order: Place, Claim, Draw. You'll do this every single turn, so let's walk through each step.

walkthrough.title: Step 1 — Place a Worker
walkthrough.text: Pick a Worker card from your hand and play it face-up on top of one of your four Crew piles. That's it — one card, one pile. You're building up your crews this way. The key thing to understand is that only the top card of each Crew pile matters when you're claiming a house, so think carefully about which pile you're stacking onto. If you don't have a Worker you want to play, you have two alternatives: you can discard a Worker card from your hand instead, or you can play a Boss card.
walkthrough.image_caption: A 3-value Plumbers card — high-value workers are best stacked on top of Crew piles

walkthrough.title: Boss Cards
walkthrough.text: Boss cards are your power plays. When you play one, you choose one of two abilities. First option — ignore Inspections this turn. If there's an Inspection card sitting on the Inspection pile, you can claim a House without paying that extra cost, and you don't have to take the Inspection card either. Second option — swap a crew member. Take the top card from one of your Crew piles and exchange it for the top card of another player's Crew pile. That player can discard a Boss card from their hand to block the swap. But here's where it gets spicy — you can then discard another Boss card to override their block, and they can counter again, back and forth until someone decides to stop. After you've used the Boss card's ability, discard it.

walkthrough.title: Step 2 — Claim a House
walkthrough.text: Now you get a chance to buy a House from the Market. Look at the three Houses available and check their costs. Each House lists what workers it needs — something like 3 Carpenters, 2 Plumbers, and 1 Electrician. To claim a House, you spend Worker cards from the tops of your Crew piles. Only the top card of each pile can be used. The numbers on your Workers need to add up to meet or exceed each worker type the House requires.
walkthrough.image_caption: Weedmoor Gardens — a 25-point house requiring 2 Carpenters, 3 Plumbers, and 2 Electricians

walkthrough.title: Claiming Example and Refill
walkthrough.text: For example, if a House needs 3 Carpenters, you could spend a 1 Carpenter and a 2 Carpenter from the tops of two different Crew piles. All spent Workers get discarded. Once you claim a House, keep it next to you — it's part of your portfolio now. Then flip new House cards from the House Deck until there are three Houses in the Market again. If an Inspection card comes up during the refill, it goes to the Inspection Pile and you keep flipping. You don't have to claim a House on your turn — sometimes it's smarter to keep building up your crews.

walkthrough.title: Inspection Cards
walkthrough.text: Inspection cards add surprise costs to house purchases. Whenever one gets flipped from the House Deck, it goes face-up on the Inspection Pile next to the Market. The top Inspection card adds its worker cost to the next House anyone claims. So if the Inspection card shows 1 Electrician and you want a House that already needs 2 Electricians, you'd need to pay 3 Electricians total. When you claim a House with an Inspection cost, you take the Inspection card too. Keep it with your claimed Houses — Inspection cards are worth points at the end of the game.
walkthrough.image_caption: Broken Water Main — a 5-point Inspection card that adds +1 Plumber to the next house claim

walkthrough.title: Step 3 — Draw Cards
walkthrough.text: After placing and optionally claiming, draw Worker cards from the deck until you have 5 cards in your hand. If the Worker Deck runs out, shuffle the discard pile to make a new deck. That's it — your turn is done. The player to your left goes next.

walkthrough.title: Trading Workers
walkthrough.text: Here's a rule that's easy to miss. Once per turn, on your turn only, you can offer to trade exactly one Worker card from your hand for exactly one Worker card from another player's hand. You can negotiate openly — say something like 'I'll trade you a 2 Electrician for a 1 Carpenter.' The other player can accept or decline. Trading is optional, but it's a great way to get the worker types you need.

walkthrough.title: Ending the Game
walkthrough.text: The game ends when the last House in the Market has been claimed and no more Houses can be flipped from the House Deck. Now count up your points. Every House card and Inspection card you've claimed has a point value printed on it. Add them all up.

walkthrough.title: The 5-Point Bonus
walkthrough.text: Here's the big twist in scoring. The player with the most 5-point House and Inspection cards gets a bonus 20 points. That's huge — it can easily swing the game. But if two or more players are tied for the most 5-point cards, nobody gets the bonus. So keep count of those 5-point cards throughout the game. The player with the most total points after the bonus wins!
walkthrough.image_caption: Villa on the Green — a 5-point house. Collecting the most 5-point cards earns a 20-point bonus

summary.title: Your Turn — Three Steps
bullets: Every turn: Place, Claim, Draw — always in this order

summary.title: Step 1 — Place a Worker
bullets: Play 1 Worker card from hand face-up onto 1 of your 4 Crew piles
Only the top card of each pile counts for claiming
Alternative: discard a Worker from hand, or play a Boss card

summary.title: Boss Cards
bullets: Option 1: Ignore Inspection costs this turn — claim a House without paying Inspection pile cost
Option 2: Swap your top Crew card with another player's top Crew card
Swaps can be blocked by discarding a Boss card (back-and-forth until someone stops)
Boss card is discarded after use

summary.title: Step 2 — Claim a House
bullets: Spend top cards from Crew piles to meet or exceed the House's worker costs
Each worker type must be met separately (Carpenters, Plumbers, Electricians)
Only top cards of Crew piles can be spent
Claiming is optional each turn

summary.title: Claiming Example and Refill
bullets: Example: House needs 3 Carpenters = spend a 1 + 2 Carpenter from two pile tops
Spent Workers are discarded
Refill Market to 3 Houses after claiming (Inspection cards go to Inspection Pile)

summary.title: Inspection Cards
bullets: Flipped Inspections go to the Inspection Pile
Top Inspection card adds its worker cost to next House claimed by anyone
When you claim, you also take the Inspection card (worth points)
Inspection costs can stack if multiple are flipped before a claim

summary.title: Step 3 — Draw Cards
bullets: Draw from Worker Deck until you have 5 cards in hand
Worker Deck empty = shuffle discard pile to form new deck

summary.title: Trading Workers
bullets: Once per turn, on your turn: offer to trade 1 hand card for 1 hand card with another player
The other player can accept or decline
Hand-to-hand only, exactly 1 for 1

summary.title: Ending the Game
bullets: Game ends when last Market House is claimed and no more Houses can be flipped
Add up point values on all claimed House cards and Inspection cards

summary.title: The 5-Point Bonus
bullets: Most 5-point House + Inspection cards = 20 bonus points
Tied for most = nobody gets the bonus
Highest total points wins

walkthrough.title: Stack Your Crews Wisely
walkthrough.text: The most common beginner mistake is spreading your workers too thin across all four Crew piles. Remember, only the top card of each pile matters when you're claiming a House. A pile with a 3 Carpenter on top is far more useful than two piles with 1 Carpenters on top — the first pile gives you 3 Carpenters worth of value from a single pile, leaving your other piles free for other worker types. When you're placing Workers, think about what the Market needs right now. Don't spread evenly — stack toward the House you want.
walkthrough.image_caption: A 1-value Electrician — low-value workers on top of piles waste valuable crew slots

walkthrough.title: The 5-Point Card Race
walkthrough.text: The 20-point bonus for having the most 5-point cards is the single biggest swing in the game. A player with three 5-point Houses and the bonus scores 35 points from those cards alone — that's often enough to win. Keep count of how many 5-point cards each player has claimed. If you're tied, neither of you gets the bonus. Sometimes it's worth claiming a smaller House just to deny someone else a 5-pointer. And remember, Inspection cards can be 5 points too — picking up a House with an Inspection card might give you two 5-point cards in one purchase.
walkthrough.image_caption: Villa on the Green — the 5-point cards are the key to the game's biggest scoring swing

walkthrough.title: Boss Card Timing
walkthrough.text: Don't waste Boss cards early. The ignore-Inspections ability is most powerful when the Inspection pile has stacked up with a heavy cost — letting you grab a great House while everyone else is scared off by the extra expense. The crew-swap ability shines when an opponent has built up a perfect top card on one of their piles — swapping your 1 Plumber for their 3 Plumber right before they can claim is devastating. Save at least one Boss card for defense too. If someone tries to swap your best crew member, having a Boss to block them is crucial in the late game.

walkthrough.title: Watch the Market and the Deck
walkthrough.text: Before you place a Worker, look at what's available in the Market. If there's a House that perfectly matches the workers you already have on top of your piles, claim it this turn instead of building up further. Also pay attention to the House Deck. When it starts getting thin, the game is almost over. That's when you should stop stockpiling and start spending — having the best crews in the game doesn't matter if there are no more Houses to claim.

walkthrough.title: Trading is Underrated
walkthrough.text: New players often forget they can trade once per turn. Trading a surplus Worker for one you actually need is essentially a free upgrade. If your hand is full of Electricians but you need Carpenters, offer a trade. Be strategic about it though — don't trade away exactly the Worker type your opponent is collecting. Pay attention to what they're building and what Houses they're eyeing in the Market.

walkthrough.title: Common Mistakes to Avoid
walkthrough.text: Here are the things that trip up most new players. First, spreading workers evenly across all four Crew piles when you should be stacking high numbers on fewer piles. Second, ignoring the 5-point bonus — that 20 points is worth more than most Houses in the game, so always keep count. Third, holding Boss cards too long. They're powerful, but if the game ends with Boss cards still in your hand, they scored you zero points. Find the right moment and use them.

summary.title: Stack Your Crews Wisely
bullets: Only top card of each Crew pile counts — stack high-value workers on top
A single 3-value worker is better than two 1-value workers in different piles
Build toward specific Houses in the Market, don't spread evenly

summary.title: The 5-Point Card Race
bullets: 20-point bonus for most 5-point cards is the biggest swing in the game
Track all players' 5-point card counts throughout
Tied for most = no one gets the bonus — sometimes deny a 5-pointer to avoid ties
Inspection cards can be 5 points too — two 5-point cards in one claim is huge

summary.title: Boss Card Timing
bullets: Ignore Inspections: best when Inspection pile has stacked heavy costs
Crew Swap: best when opponent has a high-value top card you need
Save at least 1 Boss for defense against swaps in the late game

summary.title: Watch the Market and the Deck
bullets: Check Market before placing — claim immediately if your crews already match
Thin House Deck = game nearly over — stop stockpiling, start spending
Best crews are worthless if no Houses remain to claim

summary.title: Trading is Underrated
bullets: Trade once per turn — swap surplus workers for types you actually need
Don't trade away the exact type your opponent is collecting
Pay attention to what Houses opponents are targeting

summary.title: Common Mistakes to Avoid
bullets: Spreading workers thin across all 4 piles instead of stacking high
Ignoring the 5-point bonus — 20 points is worth more than most Houses
Holding Boss cards too long — they're 0 points in your hand at game end

entries.definition: One of three worker types. Cards valued 1, 2, or 3. There are 12 of each value (36 total). Used to pay the Carpenter cost when claiming Houses.
One of three worker types. Cards valued 1, 2, or 3. There are 12 of each value (36 total). Used to pay the Plumber cost when claiming Houses.
One of three worker types. Cards valued 1, 2, or 3. There are 12 of each value (36 total). Used to pay the Electrician cost when claiming Houses.
The number printed on a Worker card (1, 2, or 3). When spending workers to claim a House, add the values of spent cards for each worker type. Values must meet or exceed the House's cost.

Special orange-bordered cards (8 in the Worker Deck). When played, choose one ability: ignore Inspection costs this turn, or swap a top Crew card with another player's. Discarded after use. Cannot be placed in Crew piles during setup.

One of two Boss abilities. Exchange the top card of one of your Crew piles for the top card of another player's Crew pile. The target may discard a Boss to block. You may discard another Boss to override, back and forth until one player stops.
One of two Boss abilities. Ignore the Inspection Pile cost this turn when claiming a House. You do not take the Inspection card when claiming.
Property cards with silly names, point values, and worker costs. Each House shows how many Carpenters, Plumbers, and Electricians are needed to claim it. There are 24 Houses in the House Deck.
The row of 3 face-up House cards available for claiming. Refilled from the House Deck after a House is claimed. When refilling, Inspection cards go to the Inspection Pile, not the Market.

Surprise cost cards shuffled into the House Deck (6 total). When flipped, placed on the Inspection Pile. The top Inspection card's worker cost is added to the next House claimed by anyone. The claiming player takes the Inspection card (worth points).
A face-up pile next to the Market where Inspection cards accumulate. Only the top card's cost applies to the next claim. The pile may have multiple cards, but only the top one adds its cost.
One of four face-up stacks of Worker cards in front of each player. Only the top card of each pile can be spent to claim Houses. Workers are placed on top during the Place step.
Spending top-of-pile Workers whose totals meet or exceed the House's cost for each worker type. All spent Workers are discarded. The House card is kept by the claiming player.
Once per turn, on your turn only, offer to swap exactly 1 Worker from your hand for exactly 1 Worker from another player's hand. The other player may accept or decline. Hand-to-hand only.
Each claimed House card has a point value printed on it. Added to total score at game end.
Each claimed Inspection card has a point value printed on it. Added to total score at game end alongside House points.
The player with the most 5-point House and Inspection cards gets a bonus 20 points. If tied for the most, nobody gets the bonus. This is the largest single scoring swing in the game.
Game ends when last Market House is claimed and no more can be flipped. Total points = House card points + Inspection card points + 5-point bonus (if applicable). Highest total wins.
